title: Thai Pad Krapow (Holy Basil Chicken Stir-Fry)

subs.name: 300 g firm tofu, crumbled, for a vegetarian version
subs.notes: reduce stir-fry time to 3 minutes; the tofu should be pressed dry first

ingredients.name: holy basil or Thai basil leaves (krapow)
visual_checks: about 25-30 leaves; fresh tulsi is an excellent substitute
ingredients.notes: about 25-30 leaves; fresh tulsi is an excellent substitute

subs.name: 20 fresh tulsi (holy basil) leaves
subs.notes: tulsi is botanically similar and gives an almost identical aroma

subs.name: 1 tbsp soy sauce plus 0.5 tsp sugar

steps.text: Using the flat of a knife or a small mortar and pestle, pound the garlic and green chillies into a rough paste — leave some texture and visible chilli seeds rather than grinding it smooth. This coarse paste fries better and gives more character than a blender paste.

Heat 2 tablespoons of oil in a wok or kadai on the highest flame available. When a drop of water flicked in the oil spits immediately, add the garlic-chilli paste and stir-fry for 30 seconds until fragrant and just turning golden at the edges.

Add the minced chicken, breaking it up immediately with a spatula into small pieces. Stir-fry on high heat for 4-5 minutes, pressing the mince against the wok surface occasionally, until no pink remains and the chicken begins to caramelise lightly at the edges.

Add fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce and sugar. Toss and stir for 1 minute until the sauces coat every piece of chicken and reduce to a glossy glaze. Taste — it should be intensely savoury, slightly sweet and fiery.

Turn off the flame and immediately add the basil leaves. Toss them through with the residual heat — about 10-15 seconds is enough to wilt and perfume them without turning them black and bitter. Remove from heat.

In a separate small pan, heat 1 tablespoon oil over medium-high heat. Crack in the eggs and fry until the whites are set and crispy at the edges but the yolk is still runny, about 2 minutes. Spoon the basil chicken over individual bowls of steamed rice and top each with a fried egg. Serve at once.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: keema → 300 g firm tofu, crumbled, for a vegetarian version (reduce stir-fry time to 3 minutes; the tofu should be pressed dry first); oyster-sauce → 1 tbsp soy sauce plus 0.5 tsp sugar (approximates the sweet-savoury balance); fish-sauce → 1.5 tbsp soy sauce; basil → 20 fresh tulsi (holy basil) leaves (tulsi is botanically similar and gives an almost identical aroma)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A cast-iron kadai on maximum gas flame replicates the wok perfectly. Minced chicken (murgh keema) is available fresh at any Indian chicken shop. Holy basil (tulsi) grows in most Indian homes and yards and is the closest plant to krapow basil — use it freely. Oyster sauce is found in the sauces aisle of most large Indian supermarkets; if unavailable, extra soy sauce with a pinch of sugar is a simple swap.
